turns.content: I'm trying to use the query macro in my new project but I'm confused why this even works this way. I thought macros were just compile-time tricks that disappear at runtime, so why does my application suddenly have a database connection string available when it starts? I just pasted DATABASE_URL into an environment variable file and I expected it to be ignored by the compiler until runtime.

The confusion comes from mixing up how macros are defined versus how they are invoked. The query macro itself is a compile-time tool that expands into SQL at build time, but the DATABASE_URL value you see in your application code is actually a runtime configuration loaded via environment variables. When you use the query macro with the sqlx crate, it generates a function that takes the database connection string as an argument at runtime. So while the macro handles the SQL generation, the actual connection details are passed to the generated function when your program executes.

Ah okay so I don't need to pass DATABASE_URL manually every time? But wait, if it's a compile-time thing why does my code still have that string in it? I thought if we use macros properly, all those manual string literals would just vanish from the final binary?

You are right to question this but there is a distinction between how you define your types and how you call them. When you define a struct or function using the query macro, you still need to provide the DATABASE_URL value at runtime so the application knows which database to connect to. The macro helps generate type-safe code for executing queries, but it does not magically remove all string literals from your program's memory footprint. However, if you use crates like env_logger or dotenv, those tools can automatically load environment variables into your config structure before your main function runs.
